title: The Water Carry
desc: Water through narrow tunnels. Bucket chains. Don't spill.

steps.text:
The lower nurseries need water. The pipes that usually supply them are blocked — a root intrusion from the swamp side has collapsed a section of clay pipe, and repairs will take days. In the meantime, the larvae need their humidity maintained.

The solution is ancient: a bucket chain. Fifty Danians standing in a line from the reservoir to the nurseries, passing buckets of water hand to hand through tunnels too narrow for carts.

You take position twenty-seven — midway through the chain, in a section of tunnel so narrow your shoulders almost touch both walls. The first bucket arrives, passed from the creature ahead of you. Full, heavy, sloshing. You pass it to the creature behind you. An empty bucket comes back. You pass it forward.

The rhythm begins.

options.label: Keep the rhythm — steady and reliable
Optimize the chain — find the bottleneck

steps.text:
Full bucket forward. Empty bucket back. Full bucket forward. Empty bucket back. The rhythm is hypnotic — a mechanical heartbeat that pulses through the entire chain. When it works, it's beautiful. Fifty creatures moving in synchronization, water flowing through stone corridors without a single pipe.

You settle into the motion. Receive, pivot, pass. The weight of a full bucket is constant — twelve pounds of water, three pounds of bucket. Your arms learn to absorb the weight and transfer it in a single motion.

An hour in, your arms burn. Two hours, they're numb. Three hours, the numbness becomes its own kind of strength — you stop feeling the weight and start being the weight, a conduit rather than a lifter.

The creature behind you starts humming. The creature ahead picks it up. The hum spreads through the chain — a wordless Hive Song, synchronized to the bucket rhythm. The sound travels through the stone, amplified by the narrow tunnel. The buckets feel lighter when the song is moving.

Four hours. The nursery reports humidity restored. The chain stops. You lower your arms and feel the blood rushing back into muscles that forgot they could rest.

The chain master counts the delivery: four hundred buckets. Four thousand eight hundred pounds of water, moved by hand, through half a mile of tunnel, without a single spill.

"Good chain," she says. It's the only praise needed.

options.label: Lower your aching arms and accept the praise

steps.text:
The chain is moving, but slowly. You watch the rhythm and spot the problem — position thirty-one, where the tunnel curves sharply. The creature at that position has to pivot a full hundred and eighty degrees to pass each bucket. Every pivot costs two seconds. Two seconds times four hundred buckets is thirteen minutes of wasted time.

You leave your position (the chain adjusts, closing the gap like a living organism) and move to the curve. There's space — barely — for two creatures to stand at the pivot point.

"You receive from the straight section," you tell the position thirty-one worker. "I'll take the pivot. We split the turn."

The double-station works. Instead of one creature doing a full pivot, two creatures do half pivots. The bucket passes through the curve in one second instead of three. The chain accelerates.

The chain master notices the speed increase from her position at the reservoir end. She walks the line, finds your modification, and nods. "Double-station at the curve. Why didn't I think of that last time?"

She adds a second double-station at position twelve, where another curve slows the flow. The chain hits peak efficiency — buckets moving so fast the rhythm becomes a blur of hands and water.

The four-hour delivery completes in three hours and twenty minutes. Forty minutes saved. The nursery gets its water sooner. The workers get to rest sooner.

"You just improved a system that's been running the same way for decades," the chain master says. "The hive adapts because individuals think."

options.label: Rest your arms and your improved chain
